caveats: a: Occupation data span employers and claim types outside the narrowed lens and do not provide task time shares.
a: Catastrophe, auto, property, liability, and specialty workflows have very different automation ceilings.
a: Existing carrier automation may already have removed some easy tasks before work reaches an independent firm.
rho: The NAIC percentages combine use, plans, and exploration and are not workflow implementation rates.
rho: The source population is insurers rather than independent adjusting firms.
rho: Regulatory acceptance and contractual audit requirements vary by state, line, and decision type.
e: No source measures the insurer-side outsourced share within the supplied LMM firm estimate.
e: Establishment classifications may not cleanly distinguish public adjusting, independent adjusting, and claims administration.
e: The supplied count is estimated and may be sensitive to margin assumptions and multi-establishment structures.
s5: The cited M&A category is much broader than NAICS 524291 and is not a direct transfer-rate observation.
s5: Small private sales and internal successions may be missing, while platform add-ons may be overrepresented.
s5: No observed owner-age distribution is available for the narrowed lens.
q: No public source quantifies billing-model mix or benefit pass-through for independent adjusting firms.
q: Catastrophe surge pricing and routine daily claims can have opposite retention economics.
q: Carrier concentration and rebid frequency vary widely by firm.
d5: Employment is not constant-price, constant-quality demand and spans carrier staff, government, and other employers.
d5: The occupational forecast embeds productivity and substitution effects that conceptually overlap with operator requirement.
d5: Catastrophe frequency and severity make year-five volume unusually volatile.
o: The narrowed LMM vendor mix may skew toward difficult claims and therefore retain operators more than the overall occupation.
o: Carrier adoption evidence does not reveal whether technology complements vendors or disintermediates them.
o: Legal accountability, licensing, and physical inspection requirements vary across jurisdictions and claim types.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Carriers, self-insured organizations, and administrators outsource daily, specialty, field, desk, or catastrophe adjusting under per-claim, fee-schedule, daily-rate, time-and-expense, or managed-service arrangements.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to insurer-side and self-insured outsourced adjusting because public adjusters represent claimants under materially different contingency-fee incentives and customer acquisition economics; combining the models would make commercial retention and operator-required demand incoherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Carrier-employed or captive adjusting units, public adjusters representing policyholders on contingency economics, pure third-party claims administrators classified in 524292, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the approximately $1–10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent adjusting firms that repeatedly investigate, appraise, document, negotiate, and settle claims for insurance carriers, self-insured organizations, or their administrators.

a.bridge: O*NET tasks for Claims Adjusters, Examiners, and Investigators are translated into a wage-weighted independent-firm task share: coverage and file review, data verification, documentation, reserve and payment entry, routine damage estimation, and correspondence are treated as substantially AI-exposed; interviews, field inspection, negotiation, litigation, and severe-claim judgment are treated as less substitutable. The occupation includes carrier employees and investigators, so applying it to outsourced adjusting likely biases exposure downward if independents handle more standardized overflow, but upward if they disproportionately receive complex field claims.
caveats: Occupation data span employers and claim types outside the narrowed lens and do not provide task time shares.
Catastrophe, auto, property, liability, and specialty workflows have very different automation ceilings.
Existing carrier automation may already have removed some easy tasks before work reaches an independent firm.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study across independent desk, field, catastrophe, liability, and specialty adjusters, separating already automated tasks from current manual work.
a.rationale: The workflow contains abundant images, forms, records, estimates, summaries, and rules-based decisions, but accountable investigation and negotiation remain central in complex, severe, disputed, or physical-loss claims.

d5.bridge: BLS projects overall claims-adjuster, appraiser, examiner, and investigator employment to decline 5% from 2024 to 2034; taking the square root of 0.95 gives an approximately 0.97 five-year factor, used as a proxy for constant-quality service demand. Employment likely understates claim-service quantity when software raises productivity, but it can overstate demand reaching independent firms if carriers automate or internalize more work; weather losses create additional upside volatility.
caveats: Employment is not constant-price, constant-quality demand and spans carrier staff, government, and other employers.
The occupational forecast embeds productivity and substitution effects that conceptually overlap with operator requirement.
Catastrophe frequency and severity make year-five volume unusually volatile.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year inflation- and severity-adjusted counts of claims assigned to independent firms, split by line, complexity, catastrophe status, and desk versus field handling.
d5.rationale: Underlying claims persist with insured exposure and loss events, but automation can reduce labor demanded for a given claim and shift routine claims away from external adjusters. Complex losses and catastrophe response support a broad range around a nearly flat base.

caveats: No source measures the insurer-side outsourced share within the supplied LMM firm estimate.
Establishment classifications may not cleanly distinguish public adjusting, independent adjusting, and claims administration.
The supplied count is estimated and may be sensitive to margin assumptions and multi-establishment structures.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of 524291 businesses in the EBITDA band, coded by customer side, contract recurrence, employee versus contractor model, owner dependence, catastrophe concentration, and transferable client agreements.
e.rationale: Many firms in the supplied LMM population should be transferable outsourced operations with repeat carrier, administrator, or self-insured relationships. The excluded share allows for public-adjuster businesses, owner-centered practices, catastrophe labor assemblers without durable customer relationships, misclassified third-party administrators, and entities lacking transferable systems or contracts.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 524291 ($6,158,275,907) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 524291 ($9,555,588,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2357 (margins.json[524]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Insurance (General) EBITDA/Sales 23.57%); revenue/employee $241,535 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 524291); class <5: 3,140 firms, avg revenue/firm $356,345 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $83,990 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 309 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,631,168 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $384,466 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 174 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,706,178 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $873,546 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 163 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,099,466 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,380,444 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 54 firms, avg revenue/firm $30,308,889 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,143,805 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $95,353,952 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $22,474,927 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 217

caveats: The narrowed LMM vendor mix may skew toward difficult claims and therefore retain operators more than the overall occupation.
Carrier adoption evidence does not reveal whether technology complements vendors or disintermediates them.
Legal accountability, licensing, and physical inspection requirements vary across jurisdictions and claim types.
o.change_evidence: Carrier and independent-adjuster cohorts measuring the share of claims fully self-served or straight-through versus externally adjusted, segmented by severity and line, with five-year technology roadmaps.
o.rationale: Routine low-severity claims can move toward self-service intake, photo estimation, automated settlement, or carrier-controlled straight-through processing. Complex, severe, disputed, specialty, field, and catastrophe claims still require an accountable operator to gather evidence, inspect losses, negotiate, document reserves, coordinate counsel, and defend decisions.

caveats: No public source quantifies billing-model mix or benefit pass-through for independent adjusting firms.
Catastrophe surge pricing and routine daily claims can have opposite retention economics.
Carrier concentration and rebid frequency vary widely by firm.
q.change_evidence: Representative master service agreements and bid histories showing billing basis, gain-sharing, fee resets, service-level penalties, technology charges, and post-automation unit economics by claim type.
q.rationale: Per-claim and fee-schedule work can retain some cycle-time and staffing savings until contract renewal, whereas daily-rate and time-and-expense work directly share or erase labor savings. Concentrated carrier procurement, service-level rebids, volume commitments, technology charges, and demands for lower unit fees should pass a substantial portion of benefits to customers.

rho.bridge: NAIC surveys of insurers report that 88% of auto, 70% of home, and 92% of health respondents use, plan to use, or explore AI or machine learning, and identify claims uses in image analysis, settlement estimation, and fraud detection. Those broad carrier adoption indicators are converted into the five-year implemented share of exposed work at independent adjusting vendors. Combining current use with plans and exploration overstates realized deployment, and carrier populations may be better resourced than LMM vendors, so the expected error is upward.
caveats: The NAIC percentages combine use, plans, and exploration and are not workflow implementation rates.
The source population is insurers rather than independent adjusting firms.
Regulatory acceptance and contractual audit requirements vary by state, line, and decision type.
rho.change_evidence: NAIC or carrier-claims surveys that separate production use from pilots by workflow, plus independent-adjuster system telemetry showing touchless and AI-assisted claim shares by severity.
rho.rationale: Carrier clients will press vendors toward digital intake, image analysis, decision support, summarization, and automated quality control. Five-year realization remains limited by fragmented systems, evidence quality, model validation, state rules, adverse-action accountability, integration with carrier platforms, and exception handling.

caveats: The cited M&A category is much broader than NAICS 524291 and is not a direct transfer-rate observation.
Small private sales and internal successions may be missing, while platform add-ons may be overrepresented.
No observed owner-age distribution is available for the narrowed lens.
s5.change_evidence: A claims-adjusting-specific five-year transaction set from PitchBook, Capital IQ, OPTIS, or industry brokers, matched to a denominator of eligible LMM firms and supplemented with owner-age and succession interviews.
s5.rationale: OPTIS reports an active broader insurance-services transaction market, including TPA transactions among 640 retail, wholesale, and TPA deals in 2025. It does not isolate claims-adjusting firms or provide a denominator, so modest five-year transfer probability is more defensible than treating the published flow as an observed rate.

principal_driver: High-volume claims workflows contain many structured cognitive tasks while severe and complex losses preserve a need for accountable human operators.
principal_weakness: Carrier-controlled automation can disintermediate vendors on routine claims and use procurement leverage to capture much of the remaining efficiency benefit.

caveats: S1 establishes the industry boundary but provides no empirical support for e = 0.75; the packet appropriately labels e as ESTIMATE and gives a broad range and specific change evidence.

The a bridge converts an occupational task inventory with no task-time or wage shares, spanning carrier employees and heterogeneous claim types, into an independent-firm exposure share; PROXY is honest, but the 0.60 base remains judgment-heavy.

The rho bridge converts insurer-level use, plans, and exploration into five-year implemented exposure at independent LMM vendors. The packet discloses the likely upward bias, but the 0.72 base is substantially more specific than the source.

The d5 employment proxy is affected by technology-driven productivity and substitution, so it is not a clean constant-quality service-demand measure and may overlap with o. The bridge discloses this limitation, and a modest correction around the stated base would not change the PRIORITY tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

S5 documents broad agent, broker, and TPA transaction activity rather than claims-adjusting transfers and has no eligible-firm denominator. The packet treats s5 as an ESTIMATE rather than an observed rate.

The q and o bases are uncited estimates about vendor contract economics and operator-required claim share. They are honestly labeled, bounded broadly, and drive MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness, but remain central diligence gaps.

sources_audited.note: The Census page supports the 524291 definition and distinguishes 524292 third-party administration, but it does not quantify the estimated 0.75 share of firms eligible under the narrower insurer-side LMM lens.

O*NET lists the cited mix of coverage review, investigation, data verification, payment and reserve entry, damage estimation, interviews, complex-claim resolution, litigation coordination, and evidence handling.

BLS reports the 5% projected 2024-2034 employment decline and 21,600 annual openings, and discusses photograph-evaluation software, AI-driven productivity, office and field work, and natural-disaster demand.
NAIC reports the stated 88% auto, 70% home, and 92% health use/plan/explore figures and identifies claims applications in accident-image analysis, settlement-value estimation, and fraud detection.

The OPTIS report states 695 total 2025 deals and 640 retail, wholesale, and TPA transactions; the packet correctly discloses that the report does not isolate claims-adjusting firms or provide their transfer-rate denominator.

sources.supports: Defines 524291 as establishments primarily engaged in investigating, appraising, and settling insurance claims and distinguishes third-party administration.
sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: Sector 52

sources.supports: Lists coverage and file review, data verification, investigation, interviews, payment and reserve entry, damage estimation, complex-claim resolution, litigation coordination, and evidence handling.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Claims Adjusters, Examiners, and Investigators

sources.supports: Reports a 5% 2024–2034 employment decline and 21,600 annual openings, documents office and field work, and explains that image-evaluation software and AI raise productivity while disasters influence demand.
sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Claims Adjusters, Appraisers, Examiners, and Investigators

sources.supports: Reports insurer use, planning, or exploration of AI or machine learning in 88% of surveyed auto insurers, 70% of home insurers, and 92% of health insurers; identifies claims uses including accident-image analysis, settlement-value estimation, and fraud detection and describes governance expectations.
sources.title: NAIC: Artificial Intelligence

sources.supports: Reports 695 announced North American insurance-distribution and related-service deals in 2025, including 640 retail, wholesale, and TPA transactions, but does not isolate claims adjusting.
sources.title: OPTIS Partners: Year-End 2025 Agent and Broker M&A Report

title: Claims Adjusting
